causes.name: Exhaust Manifold / Header Leak
causes.description: A leak upstream of the O2 sensors introduces extra oxygen, causing the sensor to read lean and falsely flag catalyst inefficiency. This is the most commonly missed cause of P0420.
supporting_evidence_template: P0420 can be triggered by exhaust leaks near O2 sensors
Exhaust smell or cold-start tick increases likelihood
what_could_be_wrong: If no exhaust smell and fuel trims are normal, this is less likely
Requires physical inspection — cannot be confirmed from OBD data alone

causes.name: Faulty Downstream O2 Sensor
causes.description: The rear O2 sensor measures catalyst efficiency. A lazy or biased sensor produces incorrect readings that mimic catalyst failure without the converter actually being bad.
supporting_evidence_template: Downstream O2 sensor failure commonly mimics P0420
High mileage increases sensor failure probability
what_could_be_wrong: If upstream/downstream voltage patterns look correct, sensor may be fine
Testing requires oscilloscope or scan tool graphing

causes.name: Catalytic Converter Degradation
causes.description: The catalyst substrate has degraded below efficiency threshold. This is the code's stated cause, but should only be concluded after ruling out the above.
supporting_evidence_template: P0420 does indicate the converter is a candidate
High mileage or history of misfires supports converter wear
what_could_be_wrong: Replacing the converter without ruling out exhaust leaks or O2 sensors is expensive and often wrong
Catalyst efficiency test needed first

causes.name: Upstream O2 Sensor Performance Issue
causes.description: A sluggish upstream sensor can cause incorrect air-fuel corrections that reduce apparent catalyst efficiency.
supporting_evidence_template: Upstream O2 performance affects catalyst efficiency readings
what_could_be_wrong: Less common than downstream sensor issues for P0420

check_first: Inspect for exhaust leaks by listening for ticking at cold start
Check for exhaust smell at startup
Read fuel trim values — high LTFT suggests upstream issue first

causes.name: Worn or Fouled Spark Plug (Cylinder 1)
causes.description: A degraded spark plug on cylinder 1 is the most common cause of a single-cylinder misfire, especially in vehicles over 60,000 miles.
supporting_evidence_template: Single-cylinder misfire typically starts with ignition components
Mileage and idle symptoms support plug wear
what_could_be_wrong: If plug looks fine, coil or injector is next
Carbon fouling on GDI engines can cause misfires not visible on plug inspection

causes.name: Failing Ignition Coil (Cylinder 1)
causes.description: Coil-on-plug systems see coil failure as a primary misfire cause. Coil failure often appears intermittently at first, especially when hot.
supporting_evidence_template: COP coil failure is a primary single-cylinder misfire cause
Heat-related intermittence supports coil over plug
what_could_be_wrong: Swap coil to another cylinder and see if misfire follows the coil — this is the definitive test

causes.name: Clogged or Failing Fuel Injector (Cylinder 1)
causes.description: A stuck or clogged injector on cylinder 1 causes lean misfire. More likely if fuel trims are elevated.
supporting_evidence_template: Injector issues can cause single-cylinder lean misfire
High fuel trims support fuel delivery issue
what_could_be_wrong: Injector issues are less common than ignition; rule out spark first

check_first: Check plug condition on cylinder 1 first
If plug looks ok, swap coil pack to another cylinder and clear codes to see if misfire follows
P0301.cascade_note: If P0420 is also present, the misfire may have damaged the catalyst. Address misfire first.

causes.name: Vacuum Leak
causes.description: Unmetered air entering after the MAF sensor causes a lean condition. This is the most common cause of P0171 and is often free or cheap to fix.
supporting_evidence_template: P0171 with elevated LTFT strongly suggests vacuum leak
Rough idle further supports air leak diagnosis
what_could_be_wrong: If LTFT is only slightly elevated, the cause may be a weak O2 sensor not a real lean condition

causes.name: Dirty or Failing MAF Sensor
causes.description: A contaminated MAF sensor under-reports airflow, causing the ECU to run lean. MAF sensors are wear items on high-mileage vehicles.
supporting_evidence_template: Dirty MAF is a leading lean-code cause
MAF value below expected range for RPM supports this
what_could_be_wrong: MAF cleaning with sensor-safe spray is a $5 first step — try this before replacement

causes.name: Weak Fuel Pump / Low Fuel Pressure
causes.description: Insufficient fuel pressure causes lean conditions, especially under load or at highway speeds.
supporting_evidence_template: Lean codes with load-based symptoms suggest fuel pressure issue
what_could_be_wrong: Requires fuel pressure gauge test to confirm — cannot be confirmed from OBD-II data alone

check_first: Check LTFT — if over +15%, start with vacuum leak inspection
MAF cleaning is free and quick — rule it out first

causes.name: Incorrect Engine Oil Viscosity
causes.description: VVT systems rely on specific oil viscosity to control cam timing. Using 5W-30 when 0W-20 is required is a common cause of P0011 and is a free fix.
supporting_evidence_template: Oil viscosity is a leading, low-cost cause of VVT cam codes
what_could_be_wrong: If oil type is correct, move to solenoid or actuator

causes.name: Faulty VVT Oil Control Solenoid
causes.description: The solenoid controls oil flow to the VVT actuator. A stuck or failed solenoid prevents proper cam timing control.
supporting_evidence_template: VVT solenoid failure is a common mid-range repair for cam timing codes
what_could_be_wrong: Solenoid replacement is mid-level DIY — often $80-150 part

causes.name: Timing Chain Stretch
causes.description: A stretched timing chain causes cam timing to drift. This is the most serious cause and is indicated by rattling on cold start.
supporting_evidence_template: Cold-start rattle with cam timing codes is a timing chain red flag
what_could_be_wrong: Timing chain failure on interference engine is catastrophic — do not delay if this is suspected

check_first: Verify oil type and level first — this is a free fix if wrong
Listen for cold-start rattle — if present, stop driving and get professional inspection
P0011.cascade_note: P0011 with engine knock = do not drive. Potential timing chain failure.

causes.name: Weak or Failing Battery
causes.description: A battery with reduced capacity causes voltage sag under load, triggering low voltage codes and potentially multiple cascade codes.
supporting_evidence_template: P0562 with multiple unrelated codes strongly suggests battery as root cause
Low battery voltage can trigger false cascade codes across systems
what_could_be_wrong: Load test the battery before chasing individual cascade codes — all other codes may disappear after battery replacement

causes.name: Failing Alternator
causes.description: An alternator not maintaining charging voltage causes progressive voltage drop while driving.
supporting_evidence_template: Alternator output can be checked with a multimeter at idle
what_could_be_wrong: Check battery first — alternator testing requires the battery to be in good condition

check_first: Battery load test first — takes 5 minutes at any auto parts store, usually free
P0562.cascade_note: P0562 is often a cascade ROOT. Multiple unrelated codes with P0562 = fix voltage first, then recheck all codes.

causes.name: Unbalanced Tire(s)
causes.description: Tire imbalance causes speed-dependent vibration, typically felt through steering wheel at 60–75 mph.
supporting_evidence_template: Speed-specific vibration in steering wheel is classic tire imbalance
what_could_be_wrong: If vibration is in seat not steering wheel, may be rear tires or driveshaft

causes.name: Worn Wheel Bearing
causes.description: A failing wheel bearing causes a growling, humming vibration that changes with vehicle speed and may intensify when turning.
supporting_evidence_template: Vibration that changes when gently weaving at highway speed suggests wheel bearing
what_could_be_wrong: Wheel bearing failure can become sudden and catastrophic — do not delay if suspected

causes.name: Worn CV Axle/Joint
causes.description: A failing CV joint causes vibration or clunking during acceleration, especially from stops or during turns.
supporting_evidence_template: CV joint symptoms typically appear during turns or acceleration
what_could_be_wrong: Pure highway speed vibration without turning is less likely CV — consider tire/bearing first

check_first: Get a tire balance first — $20-40 and eliminates the most common cause
Check for uneven tire wear pattern
NO_CODE_VIBRATION.cascade_note: OBD-II cannot detect any of these conditions. This diagnosis is based entirely on symptoms.

causes.name: Worn Brake Pads
causes.description: Brake pads at or below minimum thickness cause increased stopping distance and may produce squealing or grinding.
supporting_evidence_template: Brake wear is the most common brake symptom cause
what_could_be_wrong: If pedal is soft or spongy, pads alone are not the issue — inspect hydraulics

causes.name: Low or Degraded Brake Fluid
causes.description: Low fluid level or moisture-contaminated fluid can cause soft pedal feel and reduced braking performance.
supporting_evidence_template: Soft pedal is a hydraulic symptom, not a pad symptom
what_could_be_wrong: Soft pedal with normal fluid level may indicate air in lines or caliper issue — requires physical inspection

check_first: Visually inspect pad thickness through wheel spokes before driving
Check brake fluid level and color
NO_CODE_BRAKE.cascade_note: ANY brake symptom involving soft pedal, pedal to floor, or pulling = DO NOT DRIVE. OBD cannot detect brake hydraulic failures.
